Minimum Slack Priority Rule (MSP):
Define the project network including all tasks and their durations.
Identify the resource constraints for each task.
Calculate the earliest start time (EST) and latest finish time (LFT) for each task using forward and backward passes.
Determine the slack for each task as LFT - EST - duration.
Sort tasks by their minimum slack values; tasks with lower slack have higher priority.
Select the task with the minimum slack that does not violate resource constraints.
Assign the selected task to the earliest possible start time without exceeding resource limits.
Update the resource availability and the schedule of the project.
Repeat steps 5 to 8 until all tasks are scheduled.
Verify the final schedule for any resource conflicts or violations of precedence constraints.
Greatest Resource Demand Priority Rule (:
Define the project network with tasks, durations, and resource requirements.
Identify the total resource availability for each type of resource.
Calculate the resource demand for each task as the sum of its resource requirements.
Sort the tasks in descending order based on their resource demand.
Initialize an empty schedule and set the current time to 0.
Select the task with the highest resource demand that has all its predecessors completed and fits within the available resources.
Allocate the required resources to the selected task and add it to the schedule at the current time.
Update the current time to the end time of the most recently scheduled task.
Repeat steps 6 and 7 until all tasks are scheduled or no more tasks can be scheduled due to resource constraints.
If not all tasks are scheduled, identify the next time period where resources become available and return to step 6.
Output the final schedule and check for any unscheduled tasks.
Most Successors Priority Rule (MSP):
Define the project network including all tasks and their durations.
Identify the resource constraints for each task.
Calculate the number of successors for each task.
Sort tasks in descending order based on the number of successors.
Select the task with the highest number of successors that does not violate resource constraints.
Assign the selected task to the earliest possible time slot without violating precedence or resource constraints.
Update the resource availability and the list of remaining tasks.
Repeat steps 5-7 until all tasks are scheduled.
Verify the solution for feasibility and optimality.
Earliest Start Time Priority Rule (EST):
Define the project network with tasks, durations, and resource requirements.
Identify the precedence relationships between tasks.
Calculate the earliest start time (EST) for each task based on its predecessors' completion times.
Sort the tasks in non-decreasing order of their ESTs.
Initialize the current time to 0 and the resource availability to the initial levels.
Select the first task from the sorted list that can be started given the current resource availability.
Allocate the required resources to the selected task and update the resource availability.
Schedule the selected task to start at the current time and end at the current time plus the task duration.
Update the current time to the end time of the scheduled task.
Repeat steps 6 through 9 until all tasks are scheduled.
Output the schedule including the start and end times for each task.
Latest Finish Time Priority Rule (LFT):
Define the project network including all tasks and their durations.
Identify the precedence relationships between tasks.
Calculate the latest finish time (LFT) for each task by working backwards from the project deadline or end task.
Sort tasks in descending order based on their LFT values.
Initialize the schedule with the earliest possible start times for tasks that have no predecessors.
Select the next task from the sorted list according to the LFT priority rule.
Check resource availability for the selected task at its earliest possible start time.
If resources are available, allocate them and schedule the task; otherwise, find the next available time slot where resources are sufficient.
Update the schedule and mark the task as completed.
Repeat steps 6 through 9 until all tasks are scheduled.
Verify the final schedule meets all precedence constraints and resource limits.
Critical Ratio Priority Rule (CR):
Define the project network including all tasks, their durations, and precedence relationships.
Identify the resource constraints for each task.
Calculate the earliest start time (ES) and latest finish time (LF) for each task using a forward and backward pass through the network.
Determine the critical ratio (CR) for each task using the formula: CR = (LF - Current Time) / Duration.
Prioritize tasks based on their CR values; the lower the CR, the higher the priority.
Allocate resources to tasks starting with the highest priority task, ensuring not to exceed resource limits.
If a tie occurs in CR values, use secondary criteria such as shortest duration or earliest ES to break the tie.
Update the schedule and resource allocation after each task is assigned.
Repeat the process until all tasks are scheduled and resources are allocated.
Review the final schedule to ensure all constraints are met and optimize if necessary.
Resource Utilization Priority Rule (RUP):
Define the project network including tasks, their durations, and precedence relationships.
Identify all resources and their availability limits.
Calculate the earliest start time (ES) and latest finish time (LF) for each task using forward and backward passes.
Determine the resource utilization for each task by dividing the task's resource requirement by its duration.
Sort tasks in descending order based on their resource utilization priority (RUP).
Select the task with the highest RUP that can start at its ES without violating resource constraints.
Assign the selected task to the earliest possible start time and update resource availability.
Repeat steps 5-7 until all tasks are scheduled.
Check for any resource conflicts and resolve them by adjusting task start times or re-evaluating the RUP order.
Output the final schedule including start and end times for each task.
Longest Processing Time Priority Rule (L:
Define the problem: Identify tasks, their durations, resource requirements, and precedence constraints.
Sort tasks in descending order based on their processing times (longest first).
Initialize an empty schedule and set the current time to 0.
For each task in the sorted list:
Check if the required resources are available at the current time.
If resources are available, allocate them to the task and add the task to the schedule with its start time as the current time.
Update the current time by adding the duration of the scheduled task.
If resources are not available, move the current time forward until they become available and then schedule the task.
Repeat the process until all tasks are scheduled.
Verify that all precedence constraints are satisfied in the final schedule.
Shortest Processing Time Priority Rule (:
Define the problem: Identify tasks, their durations, resource requirements, and precedence constraints.
Create a list of all tasks with their respective durations and required resources.
Sort the tasks based on their processing time in ascending order, ensuring that precedence constraints are respected.
Initialize the schedule: Set the current time to 0 and create an empty schedule.
Check resource availability: For each task, verify if the required resources are available at the current time.
Schedule the task: If resources are available, assign the task to the schedule at the current time and update resource availability.
Update the current time: Move the current time forward by the duration of the scheduled task.
Repeat steps 5-7 until all tasks are scheduled.
Resolve conflicts: If multiple tasks have the same shortest processing time, prioritize them based on additional criteria such as earliest finish time or criticality.
Output the final schedule: Present the schedule showing the start and end times of each task.
Random Priority Rule (RPR):
Initialize the project network with tasks and their dependencies.
Assign a random priority number to each task.
Sort the tasks based on their assigned priority numbers, while respecting the precedence constraints.
Select the task with the highest priority that can start immediately based on resource availability and precedence constraints.
Allocate resources to the selected task and schedule its start and end times.
Update the resource availability and the status of the project network.
Repeat steps 4-6 until all tasks are scheduled.
Return the final schedule of the project.
